statement.markdown: Given an integer n, return all the divisors of n and sort them in ascending order. A divisor of an integer n is an integer that has a remainder of zero when divided by n.

hints.text: You can start by initializing an empty list to store the divisors of the number.
To check if a number is a divisor, you can use a loop to iterate over all numbers from 1 to n (inclusive).
Inside the loop, you can use the modulus operator (%) to check if the current number is a divisor of n.
If the remainder of n divided by the current number is zero, it means the current number is a divisor, so add it to the list.
Finally, sort the list of divisors in ascending order and return it.

editorial.title: Finding All Divisors Efficiently
editorial.intuition: The key insight is that divisors come in complementary pairs whose product equals the target number. By scanning only up to the square root, we capture both members of each pair in one pass, eliminating the need to test every integer up to n.
editorial.bruteForce: A naive approach would iterate over every integer from 1 to n, checking divisibility. While this guarantees correctness, its O(n) time is prohibitive for n as large as 10,000, especially when multiple test cases are involved. The brute force method also consumes O(1) extra space beyond the output list.
editorial.betterSolution: By observing that if d divides n then n/d is also a divisor, we only need to search up to sqrt(n). For each divisor found, we immediately add its complement, ensuring all divisors are collected with far fewer iterations.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal algorithm runs in O(sqrt(n)) time and O(sqrt(n)) space. It iterates d from 1 to floor(sqrt(n)). If n % d == 0, d and n/d are appended to a list. After the loop, the list is sorted and printed. This matches the known complexity and produces the correct divisor set.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Every divisor d of n satisfies d ≤ sqrt(n) or d ≥ sqrt(n). If d ≤ sqrt(n), the algorithm finds it directly. If d > sqrt(n), then n/d < sqrt(n) and the algorithm will encounter n/d earlier, adding d as its complement. Thus all divisors are included exactly once. Sorting ensures the output order is ascending.
editorial.dryRun: For n=28: sqrt(28)=5. The loop checks d=1 (adds 1 and 28), d=2 (adds 2 and 14), d=3 (no), d=4 (adds 4 and 7), d=5 (no). The list becomes [1,28,2,14,4,7], which after sorting yields [1,2,4,7,14,28].
commonMistakes: Assuming the first token is always the number and ignoring a possible test case count; this leads to wrong input parsing.
Adding both d and n/d without checking for equality, causing duplicate entries for perfect squares.
Failing to sort the final list, resulting in unsorted output.
Using integer division incorrectly, which can produce wrong complements.
Implementing the loop up to n instead of sqrt(n), missing the performance benefit.
Not handling the edge case n=1, which has only one divisor.

functionSignature.java: public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException

solutions.python:
import sys
it = iter(sys.stdin.read().split())
n = int(next(it))
divisors = [i for i in range(1, n + 1) if n % i == 0]
print(len(divisors))
for d in sorted(divisors):
    print(d)
